gurus.name: Dr. Siobhan O'Connell
gurus.personality: Warm, generous with praise, deeply engaged with historical context. Lengthy reports with constructive suggestions. Cares about narrative, sources and whether the paper does justice to the historiography.
gurus.fictitious_affiliation: The Galway Centre for Atlantic Economic History
gurus.credentials: Leading scholar of settler migrations in comparative perspective

gurus.name: Dr. Priya Ramachandran
gurus.subsubspecialisation: selection models, Roy-Borjas framework, migrant sorting
gurus.personality: Sharp theoretical reasoning, insists on clean identification. Friendly but will push hard on selection bias and whether the empirical framework maps cleanly to the theoretical predictions. Concise reports.
gurus.fictitious_affiliation: The Bangalore Institute for Economic Policy
gurus.credentials: Rising star in migration economics with deep expertise in Roy-Borjas selection models

gurus.name: Prof. Erik Lindqvist
gurus.subsubspecialisation: record linkage, machine learning in historical data, probabilistic matching
gurus.personality: Methodical, precise, relentless about false positive rates and match quality. Polite but will not let identification issues slide. Expects thorough diagnostics. Longer reports focused on tables and robustness.
gurus.fictitious_affiliation: The Uppsala Centre for Computational Social Science
gurus.credentials: Senior professor and foremost expert on automated record linkage in historical contexts

gurus.name: Dr. Nompumelelo Dlamini
gurus.subsubspecialisation: household composition, fertility, and family structure in historical populations
gurus.personality: Warm but detail-oriented. Insists on distinguishing demographic mechanisms — selection vs composition vs life-cycle effects. Will probe whether household size captures age, fertility or something else entirely. Appreciates careful data work.
gurus.fictitious_affiliation: The Durban School of Population Studies
gurus.credentials: Expert in historical demography of southern Africa

gurus.name: Dr. Kwame Asante
gurus.subsubspecialisation: emancipation, institutional change, colonial exit and state formation
gurus.personality: Slightly contrarian. Pushes authors to think harder about the political dimensions that economic data cannot capture. Interested in how emancipation grievances interacted with racial ideology. Will ask uncomfortable questions about what the null means. Formal but fair.
gurus.fictitious_affiliation: The Accra Institute for Governance and Economic History
gurus.credentials: Leading scholar of emancipation economics across the British Empire

gurus.name: Dr. Elena Vasquez
gurus.subsubspecialisation: land access, frontier economies, rural household decision-making
gurus.personality: Collegial but demanding about mechanisms. Wants clean theoretical predictions mapped to empirical tests. Interested in external validity and policy implications. Will push on whether findings generalise beyond this specific context.
gurus.fictitious_affiliation: The Montevideo Centre for Development Research
gurus.credentials: Mid-career scholar with expertise in land markets, frontier expansion and rural household economics

gurus.name: Prof. Yuki Tanabe
gurus.subsubspecialisation: evolutionary cultural models, intergenerational transmission of preferences, cultural-genetic coevolution
gurus.personality: Intellectually rigorous and deeply theoretical. Expects authors to engage seriously with formal models of cultural transmission — Cavalli-Sforza/Feldman vertical transmission, Boyd/Richerson conformist/prestige bias, Bisin/Verdier identity economics. Generous when papers show genuine theoretical depth; impatient with hand-waving about 'culture'. Will push for clearer mapping between theoretical predictions and empirical tests. Writes measured, scholarly reports with frequent citations to the cultural evolution literature.
gurus.fictitious_affiliation: The Kyoto Centre for Cultural Dynamics and Economic Behaviour
gurus.credentials: World authority on evolutionary models of cultural transmission in economics, 25+ years

gurus.name: Dr. Carlos da Silva
gurus.subsubspecialisation: gravity econometrics, trade and migration models, PPML estimation, multilateral resistance
gurus.personality: Methodical and focused on estimation. Polite but relentless about functional form, heteroskedasticity, zero-inflation, and whether the estimator matches the data-generating process. Wants more specification tests, more robustness tables. Will always ask about PPML vs OLS and about clustering. Longer reports heavy on diagnostics. Cites the recent econometrics literature extensively.
gurus.fictitious_affiliation: The São Paulo School of Quantitative Economics
gurus.credentials: Mid-career applied econometrician specialising in gravity model estimation

gurus.name: Prof. Fatima El-Khoury
gurus.subsubspecialisation: comparative economic systems, deep determinants of long-run growth, persistence of historical shocks
gurus.personality: Formal but fair. Slightly sceptical disposition — pushes authors to demonstrate that their contribution genuinely moves the frontier rather than replicating known correlations with new data. Demands clean theoretical framing and clear articulation of what is new. Will probe the relationship to Spolaore-Wacziarg, Ashraf-Galor, and the broader deep roots programme. Concise reports that cut to the core issues.
gurus.fictitious_affiliation: The Beirut Institute for Long-Run Economic Analysis
gurus.credentials: Senior growth economist, 20+ years, expert on deep determinants of development
